raw.objective: “to estimate phenobarbital absolute bioavailability from routine clinical data collected in neonates and young infants using a population pharmacokinetic approach”
raw.covariates: Tested: PNA, GA, SEX, WT, PHB dose Included: WT on CL and Vd
raw.observations: Bioavailability in neonates and infants seems to be reduced to less than 50%, while it is considered practically 100% in adults. The authors ascribe this difference to pH levels in infant stomachs. The model seems unbiased, but not very accurate.
raw.dose: Multiple IV and oral doses in patients
raw.structure: 1 compartment with first order absorption and elimination
raw.parameter_values: theta1=0.191 theta2=44.6 theta3=0.489 theta4=50 omega1=0.0273 omega2=0.219 omega3=0.144 sigma1=7.22
raw.validation: Yes (bootstrap of 1000) / Yes: validated in 35 neonates (PMID 28407406)

structural_model.description: 1 compartment with first order absorption and elimination

study.dose: Multiple IV and oral doses in patients
study.objective: “to estimate phenobarbital absolute bioavailability from routine clinical data collected in neonates and young infants using a population pharmacokinetic approach”

covariates.raw: Tested: PNA, GA, SEX, WT, PHB dose Included: WT on CL and Vd

normalization.omega: Values labelled OMEGA in the appendix are retained as NONMEM variances; no CV reconversion is applied.
normalization.eta: ETA distribution is inferred only when the appendix equation explicitly uses exp(ETA) or additive ETA.
normalization.source_text: The appendix row is preserved verbatim in raw; normalized fields never replace it.
